narrative:
There is a temperature inversion this morning with valleys cooler than mountain tops. Temperatures will balance out to their usual differences by the afternoon and be quite warm. Warm and dry conditions continue through the week with plenty of 5 golf ball weather. Record high temperatures are possible Wednesday and Thursday.

The RaysWeather.Com Photo Contest is underway for photos to appear in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar. We are open for submissions through April 15. We broke all records with 2026 calendar sales and look to top that with the 2027 calendar — it's our 24th calendar. Come on! Hit us with your best shot!

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Forecast Discussion Valleys this morning are cooler than mountain tops due to a strong temperature inversion. The slightly cooler air from yesterday is replaced by warmer air today as winds turn to the south so temperatures will balance out to their normal differences this afternoon. High pressure at the surface and aloft over the western Atlantic extends into the Southeast and will keep our weather warm and dry for the week. Record high temperatures are possible Wednesday and Thursday. Over the weekend, a cold front will make a valiant attempt to move through but the ridge will not give up easily. It appears any rain associated the front will be limited to isolated showers and thundershowers Friday and Saturday afternoons and evenings. There is no significant widespread rain in sight and the very dry conditions continue. Cooler temperatures should move in Sunday. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Wayne Presnell Meteorologist PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Just a tree, some sky, and MacRaes Peak at a spot along the hiking trail. Submitted by Robert Mehnert. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights An Update On Spring's Colors Folks, we made it through another winter. This isn’t to say that snowflakes can’t still fly (in fact, April almost always brings at least a light high-elevation event). But, we’ve logged several days in the 70s across the mountains and 80s in the Foothills over the past month or so, and there are leaves and blooms galore in the southern Foothills. More noticeable spring color is limited to areas below 3000 feet or so at the time of this writing (April 10th), but notable progress is being made to our south. Our own Jack Kendrick took the cover image around 1300 feet in elevation in far northern South Carolina on April 4th, just a couple miles from the state line. It is a surefire sign that spring is on its way. Lime greens and dusty yellows are a welcome sight for spring and summer lovers further south. Meanwhile, buds are more plentiful in the mountains, but trees remain bare for many of us. After such an incredibly warm run this March, you may be wondering how that affects the status of spring and its progress up the mountain. Well, thankfully, there’s an index for that! The National Phenology Network utilizes on-the-ground observations of plant species that are usually the first to show their leaves (namely honeysuckle and lilac) to track the progression of spring. You may be thinking as you glance out the window, depending on your location, “Those trees still look awfully bare to me.” They certainly are, but the First Leaf Index does not use our trees to track spring. Keep that in mind when viewing this map: This map shows how early or late spring is at a given location. For much of the Carolinas, there is actually a bit of a lag this year compared to others, perhaps due to the rather dry winter we have had, but the mountains are bright red – indicating an early leaf-out of the first spring indicator species. This certainly owes to the warm middle of February and warm March, despite the drought conditions that persist over our area. So, while you may not be seeing the results you think of when someone says “spring has arrived,” rest assured that it is moving quickly, and we are less than a month away from areas below 4000 feet seeing widespread spring fever. Here’s a rough guideline of what to expect from the next several weeks: April 12-18 | Lower valleys of Southwestern NC begin to leaf out, with noticeable green confined to 3000’ or lower. Foothills spring is in full swing, with blooms and trees greening up. April 19-25 | Buds can be found up to 4000-5000’. Many trees in the Foothills are almost completely leafed out, and High Country valleys are showing more spring. April 26-May 2 | First greens progress up to roughly 4000’. Low elevation wildflowers are starting to really get going, but the highest elevations are just getting some of their first buds. May 3-9 | All elevations have signs of spring, with lower valleys and Foothills trees just about onto summer status. Mountain laurel is plentiful below 4000’. May 10-16 | Pink Catawba rhododendron blooms are widespread below 3000’, with some higher than that. Above 5000’, spring colors are peaking, with transition to fully green not far behind. May 17-23 | Blooms can be found at most elevations, but are still limited at highest spots. High Country valleys are leafed out. May 24-30 | The last of the deciduous trees above 5000’ are leafed out, with only blooms left to progress upward. Summer is underway. The animation below shows (roughly) weekly images at 12:00PM from mid-April to June 1st, 2025 courtesy of the National Park Service webcam on Purchase Knob (5086’), just inside the Great Smoky Mountains in Haywood County. To get the best feel for the progress of spring, watch the trees on the hillside in the foreground. Apr 10, 2026 WWR? Todd Island Park WWR? Todd Island Park. For more information, visit https://toddnc.org/. Apr 10, 2026 March 2026 Recap Swings of Spring As it has a tendency to do, March brought out the hats, gloves, coats, t-shirts, shorts, and flip-flops in the same week. We had 50 degree swings in 24 hours, upper 80s in the Foothills, several inches of snow at the high peaks, rumbling thunderstorms, frosts, and even a weak tornado in Mars Hill! Suffice it to say, the tradition of wild springtime swings was upheld, and then some. The early part of the month, through the 10th, was mostly quiet beyond the ups & downs of temperatures. However, a rather active mid-month period began with a remarkably sharp cold front on the 12th. Beech Mountain recorded a temperature of 71.0 degrees on the 11th just before 3PM, and followed that up with a reading of 21.7 degrees just after noon on the 12th - nearly 50 degrees in under 24 hours! 2 to 3 inches of snow for the favored high elevations accompanied this incredible front, the first of only two notable snow events anywhere in the region during the month. Another heavier event followed on the 16th, with 4 to 6 inches at high elevations and 1 to 3 inches in the valleys. Following this active period, the final two weeks of the month were fairly quiet, with a few of the first pop-up thunderstorms of 2026 taking shape at times. Take Boone for example. If you were to draw a line connecting the top of the high temperature bars in the graph below, you'd probably be able to make a decent roller coaster out of it. The mid-month cold snap stands out, but a remarkable number of days made runs into the 60s and 70s. Local Details The prevailing storyline of the beginning of spring is the warmth and dryness we continue to experience. Many locations across the entire Southeastern U.S. experienced a top 5 (or even top 3) warmest March on record, by average temperature throughout the entire month. In tandem with that, much of the area is experiencing a notable level of drought, due to 5 consecutive months of below normal precipitation. The tables below tell the story well:

These are remarkable numbers for an entire month. It is rare to see these sorts of anomalies on the order of 8 to 12 degrees! Hickory fell just shy of a new record for average high temperature in March, with the record of 70.1F (set in 2012) standing strong by just under a degree. The Asheville Airport set three new daily record high temperatures on the 6th, 10th, and 11th. The warmth of the above table is reflected in the cover image for this article.

Most stations fell shy of 50 to 60 percent of average precipitation for March, and the drought monitor has reflected this lack of soaking rains. Springtime is windy in the mountains, and dry grounds and winds do not mix well as far as wildfire danger. A few fires did manage to break out, but thankfully nothing especially widespread developed in March. Below is our anomalous lack of rain in map form, with SWNC and the Foothills worse off than much of the Western Escarpment vicinity. This dry and warm March may cause some fire issues later on as we await the transition into the summertime thunderstorm season. As of the morning of posting this article, there is severe and extreme drought developing in the Southern Appalachians. These maps are courtesy of NOAA.
